title: Coexistence of dipolar and quadrupolar higher-order topology
authors: Konstantin Rodionenko, Maxim Mazanov, Maxim A. Gorlach
abstract: Two-dimensional higher-order topological insulators are typically classified either as dipolar or quadrupolar depending on the relevant invariant. These two classes were previously considered non-overlapping. Here we put forward an example system exhibiting dipolar and quadrupolar higher-order topology simultaneously, suggest its implementation using the arrays of laser-written evanescently coupled optical waveguides and support our conclusions by the full-wave numerical simulations.
summary: This paper demonstrates a physical realization, using coupled optical waveguides, that supports a mixed higher-order topological insulator exhibiting both dipolar and quadrupolar topological characteristics simultaneously. By analyzing the system's Hamiltonian and using advanced numerical techniques, the authors confirm the coexistence of these two distinct topological invariants in a single structure. This provides a crucial platform for engineering complex topological states in integrated photonics.
main_problem: The paper addresses the theoretical tension in higher-order topological insulators (HOTIs) regarding the non-overlapping nature of dipolar and quadrupolar bulk moments, aiming to demonstrate a system exhibiting both simultaneously.
main_result: The authors propose and analyze a model that successfully hosts a mixed dipolar-quadrupolar higher-order topological phase, confirmed by observing both corner and edge localized states.
method: The study employs Bloch Hamiltonian formulations, nested Wilson loop techniques, and full-wave numerical simulations to calculate topological invariants and analyze edge/corner spectra.
model_or_system: The physical platform is suggested to be arrays of laser-written evanescently coupled optical waveguides. The model is based on a two-orbital or single-orbital meta-atom lattice, approximated by generalized Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chains.
key_observables: Quantized bulk quadrupole moment ($q_{xy}$), bulk polarization ($\mathbf{P}$), corner-localized modes, and edge-localized modes.
important_parameters: Coupling strengths ($\kappa, \gamma, \Delta$), lattice geometry parameters ($J', \alpha$), and disorder strengths ($\delta eta, \kappa, \gamma, \Delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximating the system with effective two-band models and assumes that the topological character can be separated into dipolar and quadrupolar subsectors via Wannier center analysis.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the construction of the two-orbital model, band structures for different topological phases, Wannier bands showing polarization, and finite-size spectra highlighting spectrally separated corner and edge states.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical conflict to proposing a specific lattice model, deriving the Hamiltonian, calculating topological invariants using Wilson loops, and finally confirming the coexistence of both topologies via numerical simulations on a finite lattice.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO/Quantum Optics as it proposes a concrete, experimentally realizable platform (optical waveguides) to host and study complex topological phases beyond simple SSH models, linking fundamental topology to photonics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic comparison of dipole and quadrupole moments arising in the collection of classical charges (left) and periodic quantum systems (right).

FIG. 2. (a) Two-orbital model (a3) obtained by combining two π/2-rotated copies (a1,a2) of the orbital quadrupole insulator36. Positive and negative mode amplitudes and couplings are shown by gray and blue, respectively. The unit cell is shaded. (b) Representative band structures for the doubly-quadrupolar phase with dimerization α = 2 and κ = γ = 0 with four-fold degenerate bands (b1) and mixed dipolar-quadrupolar phase at κ = γ = ∆(b2). Gray areas highlight the topological bandgaps, while inset shows the first Brillouin zone. (c) Wannier bands νx(ky) and νy(kx) obtained via Wilson loops calculation along kx (c1) and ky directions (c2). The quadrupolar (dipolar) Wannier subsectors are...

FIG. 3. (a) The modes of a single four-site meta-atom. Couplings J are shown as dotted links. At energy −J, there is a degenerate doublet of quadrupolar (d-like) and dipolar (px+y-like) modes, while at +J – a doublet of monopolar (s-like) and orthogonal dipolar (px−y-like) modes. (b) The single-orbital model in the mixed dipolar-quadrupolar HOTI phase near the ±J energy, where J′, αJ′ ≪J. Edge (corner) states are highlighted schematically in blue (red). (c) The model disassembles into uncoupled quasi-1D “snake”-like generalized SSH chains with alternating nontrivial and trivial topology in the neighboring chains. Each quasi-1D chain contains two practically decoupled spectra formed by...

FIG. 4. (a1) Geometry of the unit cell for the optical waveguide lattice in full-wave COMSOL simulation. Waveguides are assumed to be elliptical with refractive index contrast δn = 0.002, semi-axes 2.2 µm and 7.4 µm. Ambient refractive index is set to n = 1.48 corresponding to borosilicate glass. (a2) The p-mode profile for the isolated waveguide. (a3) Possible sequential fabrication method via the femtosecond laser writing technique from the two facets of the glass wafer. (b) Numerically calculated lower (b1) and upper (b2) band structures. (c1,c2) Corresponding numerical spectrum for a finite 4 × 4-unit-cell lattice, with localized corner states amplitude profiles shown on the right.
